1:
"And the LORD said unto Noah, Come thou and all thy house into the ark; for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation." The witnesses note God's command to Noah to enter the ark, with his house. On "Come thou and all thy house into the ark," the grammatical voices note the invitation. Ellicott and Gill note that "Come thou and all thy house into the ark" — God invites Noah and his whole household to enter the ark; "Come" (rather than "go") suggests God is, as it were, in the ark, calling Noah into the refuge with him; the gracious call to enter the place of safety before the flood; the whole household is to be preserved.

On "all thy house," the reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the household. Calvin and Henry note that "all thy house" — Noah's family (his wife, sons, and sons' wives, the eight souls, 1 Peter 3:20) is included in the salvation; God saves not Noah only but his house; the covenant grace extends to the family of the righteous; the household preserved with him.

On "for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the ground: "for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation" — God declares Noah righteous before him, amid a corrupt generation; Noah's righteousness (the fruit of grace and faith, 6:8-9; Hebrews 11:7) is the reason given for his preservation; the one righteous man, found amid universal corruption, is saved; God's regard to the righteous in the day of judgment (2 Peter 2:5, 9). The verse displays God's command to Noah to enter the ark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the invitation — "Come thou and all thy house into the ark," God calling Noah and his household into the refuge (Ellicott, Gill); "all thy house," the family preserved, the eight souls (1 Peter 3:20, Calvin, Henry); "for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation," Noah's righteousness the ground of his preservation (Hebrews 11:7; 2 Peter 2:5, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the called Noah. The verse marks the call: come into the ark. The verse displays the household: all thy house. The verse displays the righteousness: Noah's. The verse displays the preservation: of the righteous. The command displays God calling the righteous Noah into the ark. The verse displays the Noah: called. The verse displays the house: saved. The verse displays the righteous: preserved. God's command to Noah to enter the ark displays the salvation of the righteous: God called Noah and all his house into the ark, for he had seen Noah righteous in that generation — the gracious call to the refuge, the righteous man and his household preserved amid the corrupt world. The verse displays the called Noah, gathering the clean and unclean (vv.2-3).
2:
"Of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens, the male and his female: and of beasts that are not clean by two, the male and his female." The witnesses note the distinction of clean and unclean beasts in the gathering. On "Of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens," the grammatical voices note the clean by sevens. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens" — Noah is to take seven (or seven pairs) of every clean beast; more of the clean animals are preserved than of the unclean (which are by twos, v.19); the greater number of clean beasts provides for sacrifice (8:20) and for man's later use.

On "the distinction of clean and unclean," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the early distinction. Calvin and Gill note that the distinction of "clean" and "not clean" beasts existed before the Mosaic law (which later codified it, Leviticus 11); the distinction, known to Noah, was probably for sacrifice (the clean being the animals fit for offering to God); the early knowledge of clean and unclean, given by God or by tradition from the beginning.

On "of beasts that are not clean by two," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the unclean by pairs: "of beasts that are not clean by two, the male and his female" — the unclean beasts are preserved by pairs (a male and female of each), sufficient to continue the kind; fewer of the unclean, since they were not needed for sacrifice; the preservation of all the kinds, clean and unclean, but more of the clean. The verse displays the distinction of clean and unclean beasts in the gathering.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the clean by sevens — "of every clean beast thou shalt take to thee by sevens," more of the clean preserved, for sacrifice and use (8:20, Keil, Ellicott); the distinction of clean and unclean existing before the law, for sacrifice (Leviticus 11, Calvin, Gill); "of beasts that are not clean by two," the unclean by pairs, to continue the kind (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered clean and unclean. The verse marks the clean: by sevens. The verse displays the unclean: by twos. The verse displays the distinction: before the law. The verse displays the purpose: sacrifice. The gathering displays more of the clean preserved, for sacrifice. The verse displays the clean: sevens. The verse displays the unclean: twos. The verse displays the distinction: early. The distinction of clean and unclean beasts in the gathering displays the early knowledge of clean and unclean: the clean beasts by sevens, the unclean by twos — more of the clean preserved for sacrifice and use, the distinction known before the Mosaic law. The verse displays the gathered beasts, with the fowls by sevens (v.3).
3:
"Of fowls also of the air by sevens, the male and the female; to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth." The very brief verse notes the fowls preserved and the seed kept alive. The grammatical voices note the birds. Gill and Calvin note that "Of fowls also of the air by sevens, the male and the female" — the clean fowls (and perhaps the birds generally) were to be taken by sevens, male and female; the birds, like the beasts, were preserved in the ark; the comprehensive gathering of the air-breathing creatures.

On "by sevens, the male and the female," the witnesses note the pairs and sevens. Gill notes that the fowls were preserved by sevens (the clean), male and female; the greater number provided for sacrifice and for the restocking of the earth; the careful preservation of the bird-kinds.

On "to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the purpose: "to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth" — the animals and birds were preserved to keep their kinds (seed) alive, that the earth might be restocked after the flood; God's purpose in the preservation was the renewal of the animal creation; the ark held the seed of all the kinds, that they might replenish the earth; the divine care for the continuance of the creation. The verse displays the fowls preserved and the seed kept alive.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the fowls — "Of fowls also of the air by sevens, the male and the female," the clean fowls preserved by sevens, male and female (Gill, Calvin); the greater number for sacrifice and restocking (Gill); "to keep seed alive upon the face of all the earth," the kinds preserved to restock the earth after the flood (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the preserved fowls. The verse marks the fowls: by sevens. The verse displays the pairs: male and female. The verse displays the purpose: keep seed alive. The verse displays the earth: restocked. The preservation displays the bird-kinds kept alive for the earth's renewal. The verse displays the fowls: preserved. The verse displays the seed: kept alive. The verse displays the kinds: renewed. The fowls preserved and the seed kept alive display the purpose of the gathering: the fowls by sevens, male and female, to keep seed alive upon the earth — the bird-kinds preserved with the beasts, that the kinds might survive and restock the earth after the flood. The verse displays the preserved kinds, before the announcement of the rain (v.4).
4:
"For yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain upon the earth forty days and forty nights; and every living substance that I have made will I destroy from off the face of the earth." The witnesses note the seven days' warning and the forty days' rain. On "For yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain upon the earth," the grammatical voices note the respite. Ellicott and Gill note that "for yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain" — God gives Noah seven days' notice before the flood; the seven days are a final respite (the last of God's longsuffering), during which Noah and the animals enter the ark; the brief, final delay before the judgment falls.

On "forty days and forty nights," the witnesses note the rain. Clarke and Gill note that the rain would fall "forty days and forty nights" — the continuous, heavy rain (with the fountains of the deep, v.11) that would cause the flood; forty days and nights of unceasing downpour; the great deluge that would cover the earth.

On "every living substance that I have made will I destroy from off the face of the earth," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the destruction: "every living substance that I have made will I destroy from off the face of the earth" — all living things (save those in the ark) would be destroyed; God repeats the sentence of universal destruction (6:7, 17); the comprehensive blotting out of all that lived on the earth; the judgment is sure and total. The verse displays the seven days' warning and the forty days' rain.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the respite — "for yet seven days, and I will cause it to rain," the seven days' final notice before the flood (Ellicott, Gill); "forty days and forty nights," the continuous rain causing the flood (Clarke, Gill); "every living substance... will I destroy," the universal destruction (6:7, 17, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the announced flood. The verse marks the respite: seven days. The verse displays the rain: forty days and nights. The verse displays the destruction: every living substance. The verse displays the judgment: sure. The announcement displays the final warning and the coming deluge. The verse displays the days: seven. The verse displays the rain: forty. The verse displays the destruction: total. The seven days' warning and the forty days' rain display the imminent flood: in seven days God would send rain forty days and forty nights, destroying every living substance — the final respite, the great deluge, and the sure, total destruction of all that lived. The verse displays the announced judgment, met by Noah's obedience (v.5).
5:
"And Noah did according unto all that the LORD commanded him." The very brief verse notes Noah's obedience in all things. The grammatical voices note the full obedience. Gill and Henry note that "Noah did according unto all that the LORD commanded him" — Noah obeyed God in all that he was commanded (as 6:22); he entered the ark with his family, gathered the animals (clean and unclean) and the food, in obedience to God; Noah's faith showed itself in complete obedience (Hebrews 11:7).

On "according unto all that the LORD commanded him," the reformed JFB and puritan Henry note the completeness. JFB and Henry note that Noah did "according unto all" — his obedience was entire and exact; he did everything as God directed, holding back nothing; the full, careful obedience of faith; Noah is the pattern of thorough, unquestioning obedience to God's word.

On the obedience, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the faith: Noah's obedience flowed from his faith — believing God's word concerning the flood (a thing not seen, Hebrews 11:7), he prepared the ark and obeyed; his obedience, against the scorn of the world and the seeming improbability of the flood, was the active faith of the righteous; the model of the believer who obeys God's word and prepares for what God has warned. The verse displays Noah's obedience in all things.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "Noah did according unto all that the LORD commanded him," Noah obeying God in all things (6:22; Hebrews 11:7, Gill, Henry); "according unto all," the entire, exact obedience (JFB, Henry); the obedience flowing from faith, the model believer (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the obedient Noah. The verse marks the obedience: all God commanded. The verse displays the completeness: entire. The verse displays the faith: active. The verse displays the model: of obedience. The obedience displays Noah doing all that God commanded. The verse displays the Noah: obedient. The verse displays the command: fulfilled. The verse displays the faith: shown. Noah's obedience in all things displays the faith that obeyed God's word: Noah did according to all that the LORD commanded him — his entire, exact obedience, the active faith (Hebrews 11:7) that obeyed God's word concerning the flood, the model of the believer who prepares for what God has warned. The verse displays the obedient Noah, entering the ark in the flood-year (vv.6-7).
6:
"And Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters was upon the earth." The very brief verse notes Noah's age at the flood. The grammatical voices note the date. Ellicott and Gill note that "Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters was upon the earth" — Noah's age, six hundred years, dates the flood; the flood came in the six hundredth year of Noah's life (v.11 gives the day); the careful chronology marks the flood as a real, dated event in history.

On "six hundred years old," the witnesses note the patriarch's age. Gill notes that Noah was six hundred years old at the flood; he lived three hundred fifty years after (9:28), to nine hundred fifty years (9:29); the long-lived patriarch witnessed the old world's destruction and the new world's beginning; his age anchors the chronology.

On "when the flood of waters was upon the earth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the flood: "the flood (mabbul) of waters was upon the earth" — the great deluge came upon the earth; the flood, long announced and prepared for, now arrives; the dating by Noah's age marks the certainty and historicity of the event; the flood is set in the framework of real time. The verse displays Noah's age at the flood.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the date — "Noah was six hundred years old when the flood of waters was upon the earth," the flood dated by Noah's age (Ellicott, Gill); "six hundred years old," the patriarch's age, anchoring the chronology (Gill); "the flood of waters was upon the earth," the deluge arriving as announced (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dated flood. The verse marks the age: 600 years. The verse displays the flood: upon the earth. The verse displays the date: anchored. The verse displays the event: historical. The dating displays the flood set in real time by Noah's age. The verse displays the Noah: 600. The verse displays the flood: come. The verse displays the chronology: marked. Noah's age at the flood displays the historicity of the event: Noah was six hundred years old when the flood came upon the earth — the flood dated by the patriarch's age, set in the framework of real time, the great deluge arriving as God had announced. The verse displays the dated flood, into which Noah enters (v.7).
7:
"And Noah went in, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him, into the ark, because of the waters of the flood." The very brief verse notes Noah and his household entering the ark. The grammatical voices note the entrance. Gill and Wesley note that "Noah went in, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him, into the ark, because of the waters of the flood" — Noah and his whole household (the eight souls, 1 Peter 3:20) entered the ark, as God had commanded (v.1); they went in "because of the waters of the flood" — in obedience and faith, taking refuge in the ark from the coming flood.

On "Noah went in, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives," the witnesses note the household. Gill notes that the eight persons — Noah, his three sons, his wife, and his sons' wives — entered the ark; the family of the righteous preserved together; the household of faith taking refuge.

On "because of the waters of the flood," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the faith: "because of the waters of the flood" — they entered in view of the coming flood, believing God's word; faith moved them to take refuge in the ark before the waters came; the obedience of faith, acting on God's warning (Hebrews 11:7, "moved with fear, prepared an ark"); the household saved by entering the refuge in faith. The verse displays Noah and his household entering the ark.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the entrance — "Noah went in, and his sons, and his wife, and his sons' wives with him, into the ark," the eight souls entering the ark as commanded (v.1; 1 Peter 3:20, Gill, Wesley); the household of the righteous preserved together (Gill); "because of the waters of the flood," entering in faith, in view of the coming flood (Hebrews 11:7, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the entering household. The verse marks the entrance: into the ark. The verse displays the household: eight souls. The verse displays the cause: the coming flood. The verse displays the faith: acting. The entrance displays Noah's household taking refuge in the ark. The verse displays the Noah: entering. The verse displays the family: with him. The verse displays the refuge: the ark. Noah and his household entering the ark display the refuge of faith: Noah, his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives went into the ark because of the flood — the eight souls entering the refuge in obedience and faith, in view of the coming waters. The verse displays the sheltered household, with the animals entering (vv.8-9).
8:
"Of clean beasts, and of beasts that are not clean, and of fowls, and of every thing that creepeth upon the earth," The very brief verse notes the classes of creatures gathered to the ark. The grammatical voices note the kinds. Gill and Ellicott note that "Of clean beasts, and of beasts that are not clean, and of fowls, and of every thing that creepeth upon the earth" — the verse lists the classes of creatures that entered the ark: clean beasts, unclean beasts, fowls, and creeping things; the comprehensive gathering of all the kinds of land-dwelling, air-breathing creatures; the animals preserved in their classes.

On "clean beasts, and... beasts that are not clean," the witnesses note the distinction. Gill notes that both clean and unclean beasts were gathered (the clean by sevens, the unclean by twos, vv.2-3); the distinction is maintained; both classes preserved, that the kinds might survive.

On "fowls, and... every thing that creepeth upon the earth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the comprehensiveness: "fowls, and... every thing that creepeth upon the earth" — the birds and the creeping things, with the beasts, make up the full range of the creatures preserved; all the kinds of air-breathing land creatures entered the ark; the comprehensive preservation of the animal creation, that it might be renewed after the flood. The verse displays the classes of creatures gathered to the ark.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the classes — "Of clean beasts, and of beasts that are not clean, and of fowls, and of every thing that creepeth upon the earth," the comprehensive classes of creatures gathered (Gill, Ellicott); both clean and unclean beasts, by their numbers (vv.2-3, Gill); "fowls, and every thing that creepeth," the full range of creatures preserved (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathered classes. The verse marks the beasts: clean and unclean. The verse displays the fowls: and creeping things. The verse displays the kinds: comprehensive. The verse displays the preservation: of all. The gathering displays all the classes of creatures entering the ark. The verse displays the beasts: gathered. The verse displays the fowls: and creepers. The verse displays the kinds: preserved. The classes of creatures gathered to the ark display the comprehensive preservation: clean beasts, unclean beasts, fowls, and creeping things — all the classes of air-breathing land creatures gathered to the ark, that the animal kinds might be preserved through the flood. The verse displays the gathered creatures, entering two by two (v.9).
9:
"There went in two and two unto Noah into the ark, the male and the female, as God had commanded Noah." The very brief verse notes the creatures entering the ark two by two. The grammatical voices note the pairs. Gill notes that "There went in two and two unto Noah into the ark, the male and the female, as God had commanded Noah" — the creatures entered the ark two by two (a male and female of each kind), as God had commanded; the basic pairs (with the additional clean by sevens, v.2) ensured the continuance of every kind; the animals came to Noah and entered the ark.

On "two and two unto Noah," the witnesses note the gathering. Gill notes that the creatures "went in... unto Noah" — they came to Noah and entered the ark; God's providence (6:20) brought the animals to the ark, by instinct and divine direction; the creatures came of themselves, not all hunted out by Noah; God's hand in gathering the kinds.

On "the male and the female, as God had commanded Noah," the grammatical-historical note the obedience and the breeding pairs: "the male and the female, as God had commanded Noah" — male and female of each kind, to propagate the species; the entrance of the pairs was as God had commanded; the careful fulfillment of God's instruction; the breeding pairs preserved to renew the kinds after the flood. The verse displays the creatures entering the ark two by two.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the pairs — "There went in two and two unto Noah into the ark, the male and the female," the creatures entering two by two, the breeding pairs (Gill); "two and two unto Noah," the animals coming to Noah by God's providence (6:20, Gill); "as God had commanded Noah," the entrance as God directed, the pairs to renew the kinds (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the entering pairs. The verse marks the entrance: two and two. The verse displays the pairs: male and female. The verse displays the providence: brought to Noah. The verse displays the command: fulfilled. The gathering displays the creatures entering the ark in pairs as commanded. The verse displays the animals: two by two. The verse displays the pairs: entering. The verse displays the kinds: preserved. The creatures entering the ark two by two display the gathering of the kinds: the creatures went in two and two, male and female, as God had commanded — the breeding pairs entering the ark by God's providence, preserved to renew the kinds after the flood. The verse displays the gathered pairs, before the coming of the waters (v.10).
10:
"And it came to pass after seven days, that the waters of the flood were upon the earth." The very brief verse notes the coming of the flood after the seven days. The grammatical voices note the fulfillment. Gill and Ellicott note that "it came to pass after seven days, that the waters of the flood were upon the earth" — after the seven days' respite (v.4), the flood came; God's word was exactly fulfilled; the waters of the flood came upon the earth at the appointed time; the certainty and punctuality of God's judgment.

On "after seven days," the witnesses note the appointed time. Gill notes that the flood came "after seven days" — exactly as God had said (v.4); the seven days of final respite ended, and the flood began; God's word is sure, fulfilled to the day; the appointed time of judgment came.

On "the waters of the flood were upon the earth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the deluge: "the waters of the flood were upon the earth" — the great deluge began (the fountains of the deep and the windows of heaven, v.11); the flood, long announced, now came; the judgment fell upon the corrupt world; the waters that would destroy all flesh came upon the earth at God's appointed time. The verse displays the coming of the flood after the seven days.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the fulfillment — "it came to pass after seven days, that the waters of the flood were upon the earth," the flood coming after the seven days, exactly as God said (v.4, Gill, Ellicott); "after seven days," the appointed time, God's word fulfilled (Gill); "the waters of the flood were upon the earth," the deluge beginning, the judgment falling (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the coming flood. The verse marks the time: after seven days. The verse displays the fulfillment: God's word. The verse displays the waters: upon the earth. The verse displays the judgment: falling. The coming displays the flood arriving at the appointed time. The verse displays the seven days: ended. The verse displays the flood: come. The verse displays the judgment: begun. The coming of the flood after the seven days displays the punctual fulfillment of God's word: after the seven days the waters of the flood came upon the earth — exactly as God had said, the respite ended and the judgment falling on the corrupt world. The verse displays the coming flood, with the breaking up of the deep (v.11).
11:
"In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, the seventeenth day of the month, the same day were all the fountains of the great deep broken up, and the windows of heaven were opened." The witnesses note the breaking up of the deep and the opening of the windows of heaven. On "In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, the seventeenth day," the grammatical voices note the precise date. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note the exact dating: "in the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, the seventeenth day of the month" — the flood is dated to the day; the precise chronology marks the flood as a real, historical event; the day the judgment began is recorded.

On "all the fountains of the great deep broken up," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the subterranean waters. Henry and Clarke note that "all the fountains of the great deep (tehom) were broken up" — the subterranean waters, the great deep, burst forth; the waters under the earth were released, flooding up from below; the breaking up of the fountains of the deep was one great source of the flood.

On "the windows of heaven were opened," the reformed Calvin, wesleyan-arminian Clarke, and grammatical-historical note the rain from above: "the windows (floodgates) of heaven were opened" — the waters above (the atmospheric/celestial waters) poured down; the heavens opened as floodgates, sending the forty days' rain (v.12); the two sources of the flood — the deep below and the heavens above — together caused the deluge; the figure of the ordered separation of waters (1:6-7) undone, the waters above and below loosed upon the earth in judgment. The verse displays the breaking up of the deep and the opening of the windows of heaven.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the date — "in the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, the seventeenth day," the flood dated to the day, a historical event (Keil, Ellicott); "all the fountains of the great deep broken up," the subterranean waters bursting forth (Henry, Clarke); "the windows of heaven were opened," the waters above pouring down, the two sources of the flood (Calvin, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the loosed waters. The verse marks the date: precise. The verse displays the deep: broken up. The verse displays the heavens: opened. The verse displays the flood: from below and above. The breaking displays the waters of the deep and the heavens loosed upon the earth. The verse displays the fountains: broken. The verse displays the windows: opened. The verse displays the deluge: begun. The breaking up of the deep and the opening of the windows of heaven display the sources of the flood: on the dated day, the fountains of the great deep were broken up and the windows of heaven opened — the waters below and above loosed upon the earth, the deluge that would cover the world begun. The verse displays the loosed waters, with the forty days' rain (v.12).
12:
"And the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights." The very brief verse notes the forty days' and nights' rain. The grammatical voices note the deluge. Gill and Clarke note that "the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights" — the rain (from the opened windows of heaven, v.11) fell continuously for forty days and forty nights; the unceasing, torrential rain, with the fountains of the deep, caused the flood to rise; the great deluge, exactly as God had said (v.4).

On "forty days and forty nights," the witnesses note the duration. Clarke and Gill note that the rain continued "forty days and forty nights" — a long, continuous downpour; the forty days mark the period of the flood's rising; the rain did not cease until the waters had covered the earth; the relentless deluge.

On the rain, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the fulfillment: the forty days' rain fulfilled God's word (v.4); the deluge, long announced, fell upon the earth; the rain from above and the deep from below together flooded the world; the judgment proceeded exactly as God had warned. The verse displays the forty days' and nights' rain.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the rain — "the rain was upon the earth forty days and forty nights," the continuous, torrential rain causing the flood (v.11, Gill, Clarke); "forty days and forty nights," the long downpour, the flood's rising (Clarke, Gill); the rain fulfilling God's word (v.4, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the deluge. The verse marks the rain: forty days. The verse displays the duration: and nights. The verse displays the flood: rising. The verse displays the fulfillment: God's word. The rain displays the relentless deluge covering the earth. The verse displays the rain: pouring. The verse displays the days: forty. The verse displays the flood: rising. The forty days' and nights' rain display the deluge: the rain fell upon the earth forty days and forty nights — the continuous, torrential downpour, with the fountains of the deep, causing the flood to rise, exactly as God had said. The verse displays the rising flood, into which Noah had entered (vv.13-16).
13:
"In the selfsame day entered Noah, and Shem, and Ham, and Japheth, the sons of Noah, and Noah's wife, and the three wives of his sons with them, into the ark;" The witnesses note Noah and his household entering the ark on the very day of the flood. On "In the selfsame day entered Noah," the grammatical voices note the timing. Ellicott and Henry note that "in the selfsame day entered Noah... into the ark" — on the very day the flood began (v.11), Noah and his household entered the ark; the entrance was exactly timed to the coming of the flood; God brought them into the refuge on the day of judgment; the precise providence of God in their preservation.

On "Noah, and Shem, and Ham, and Japheth... and Noah's wife, and the three wives of his sons," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the eight souls. Calvin and Gill note that the eight persons — Noah, his three sons, his wife, and his sons' wives (1 Peter 3:20) — entered the ark together; the whole household of the righteous preserved; the named family, the remnant saved from the flood, through whom the race would be renewed.

On "with them, into the ark," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the refuge: "with them, into the ark" — the family entered the ark together, the place of safety; God's grace gathered the household into the refuge on the day of the flood; the eight souls, by faith and obedience, took shelter in the ark from the waters; the salvation of the righteous remnant. The verse displays Noah and his household entering the ark on the day of the flood.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the timing — "in the selfsame day entered Noah... into the ark," the household entering on the very day of the flood (v.11, Ellicott, Henry); "Noah, and Shem, and Ham, and Japheth... and Noah's wife, and the three wives," the eight souls preserved (1 Peter 3:20, Calvin, Gill); "into the ark," the family gathered into the refuge (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the entering household. The verse marks the day: the selfsame. The verse displays the household: eight souls. The verse displays the entrance: into the ark. The verse displays the providence: precise. The entrance displays the family preserved in the ark on the day of judgment. The verse displays the Noah: entering. The verse displays the eight: souls. The verse displays the refuge: the ark. Noah and his household entering the ark on the day of the flood display the precise preservation of the righteous: on the very day the flood began, Noah, his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives entered the ark — the eight souls gathered into the refuge by God's providence, saved on the day of judgment. The verse displays the sheltered household, with all the kinds (vv.14-16).
14:
"They, and every beast after his kind, and all the cattle after their kind, and every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth after his kind, and every fowl after his kind, every bird of every sort." The very brief verse notes all the kinds of creatures entering the ark with Noah. The grammatical voices note the comprehensive entry. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "they, and every beast after his kind, and all the cattle... and every creeping thing... and every fowl after his kind, every bird of every sort" — with Noah's household entered all the kinds of creatures: beasts, cattle, creeping things, and fowls (every bird of every sort); the comprehensive gathering of all the air-breathing land creatures, each after its kind, into the ark.

On "every beast after his kind... after their kind... after his kind," the witnesses note the kinds. Gill and Ellicott note the repeated "after his/their kind" — each species distinct, entering by kind; the preservation of the distinct kinds (the species of ch. 1); the ark held representatives of all the kinds, that they might survive and be renewed.

On "every bird of every sort," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the completeness: "every bird of every sort (every winged creature)" — the comprehensive preservation of all the kinds of birds and winged creatures; nothing of the air-breathing land creation was left to perish without preservation in the ark; the full range of the kinds gathered; God's care for the whole creation. The verse displays all the kinds of creatures entering the ark with Noah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the entry — "every beast after his kind... every creeping thing... every fowl after his kind, every bird of every sort," all the kinds of creatures entering with Noah (Keil, Ellicott); the repeated "after his kind," the distinct kinds preserved (Gill, Ellicott); "every bird of every sort," the comprehensive preservation of all the kinds (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the entering kinds. The verse marks the beasts: after their kind. The verse displays the creeping things: and fowls. The verse displays the birds: every sort. The verse displays the kinds: all preserved. The entry displays all the kinds entering the ark with Noah. The verse displays the creatures: entering. The verse displays the kinds: distinct. The verse displays the preservation: complete. All the kinds of creatures entering the ark with Noah display the comprehensive preservation: with Noah entered every beast, cattle, creeping thing, and fowl, each after its kind, every bird of every sort — all the air-breathing land creatures gathered into the ark, that the kinds might survive the flood. The verse displays the gathered kinds, two by two (v.15).
15:
"And they went in unto Noah into the ark, two and two of all flesh, wherein is the breath of life." The very brief verse notes the creatures entering by pairs, all that had the breath of life. The grammatical voices note the pairs. Gill and Clarke note that "they went in unto Noah into the ark, two and two of all flesh, wherein is the breath of life" — the creatures entered the ark by twos (the basic pairs, with the clean by sevens, v.2), all flesh that had the breath of life; the air-breathing land creatures entered to be preserved; the pairs of all the kinds gathered.

On "two and two of all flesh," the witnesses note the breeding pairs. Gill notes that the creatures went in "two and two" — male and female of each kind, to continue the species; the pairs preserved the kinds; the comprehensive entry of the breeding pairs of all flesh.

On "wherein is the breath of life," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the living creatures: "wherein is the breath of life" — all the creatures that breathe (the air-breathing land animals and birds); the breath of life marks the living creatures preserved; those that breathed were gathered into the ark, while those outside (with the breath of life) would perish (vv.21-22); the preservation of the breathing kinds. The verse displays the creatures entering by pairs, all that had the breath of life.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the pairs — "they went in unto Noah into the ark, two and two of all flesh," the creatures entering by pairs, the breeding pairs (Gill, Clarke); "two and two," male and female to continue the kinds (Gill); "wherein is the breath of life," the breathing creatures preserved (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the entering pairs. The verse marks the entrance: two and two. The verse displays the flesh: all that breathe. The verse displays the breath: of life. The verse displays the pairs: preserved. The entry displays the breathing kinds entering the ark in pairs. The verse displays the creatures: two by two. The verse displays the breath: of life. The verse displays the kinds: gathered. The creatures entering by pairs, all that had the breath of life, display the preservation of the breathing kinds: they went in two and two, all flesh in which is the breath of life — the breeding pairs of all the air-breathing creatures gathered into the ark, preserved while those outside would perish. The verse displays the gathered pairs, shut in by God (v.16).
16:
"And they that went in, went in male and female of all flesh, as God had commanded him: and the LORD shut him in." The witnesses note the creatures entering male and female, and the LORD shutting Noah in. On "they that went in, went in male and female of all flesh, as God had commanded him," the grammatical voices note the obedient entry. Ellicott and Gill note that "they that went in, went in male and female of all flesh, as God had commanded him" — the creatures entered male and female, as God had commanded; the entry was exactly as God directed; the breeding pairs of all flesh gathered; the careful fulfillment of God's instruction in the preservation of the kinds.

On "as God had commanded him," the witnesses note the obedience. Gill notes that all was done "as God had commanded him" — the gathering and entry of the creatures fulfilled God's command exactly; Noah's obedience and God's providence together accomplished the preservation; the divine instruction carried out to the letter.

On "the LORD shut him in," the reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Clarke note the divine sealing: "the LORD shut him in" — God himself closed the door of the ark; the single door (6:16) was shut by God's own hand; the LORD sealed Noah and his household safely within, and shut out the world; the figure of God's protecting care (Noah secured within) and of the closing of the door of mercy (the world shut out); after God shut the door, none could enter or leave; the ark, sealed by God, bore Noah safely through the flood. The verse displays the creatures entering male and female, and the LORD shutting Noah in.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the entry — "they that went in, went in male and female of all flesh, as God had commanded him," the creatures entering in pairs as commanded (Ellicott, Gill); all done as God commanded, the obedient fulfillment (Gill); "the LORD shut him in," God himself closing the door, sealing Noah safely and shutting out the world (Calvin, Clarke). The verse displays the shut ark. The verse marks the entry: male and female. The verse displays the obedience: as commanded. The verse displays the door: shut by God. The verse displays the protection: Noah sealed in. The act displays God shutting Noah safely into the ark. The verse displays the creatures: entered. The verse displays the LORD: shutting. The verse displays the door: closed. The creatures entering male and female, and the LORD shutting Noah in, display God's protecting care: the creatures entered in pairs as God commanded, and the LORD shut Noah in — God himself sealing the door, securing Noah and his household within the ark, and shutting out the doomed world. The verse displays the sealed ark, lifted by the rising waters (v.17).
17:
"And the flood was forty days upon the earth; and the waters increased, and bare up the ark, and it was lift up above the earth." The witnesses note the rising flood lifting up the ark. On "the flood was forty days upon the earth; and the waters increased," the grammatical voices note the rising waters. Gill and Henry note that "the flood was forty days upon the earth; and the waters increased" — during the forty days (the rain and the deep, vv.11-12), the waters rose and increased; the flood grew, covering more and more of the earth; the waters steadily mounted as the deluge continued.

On "bare up the ark, and it was lift up above the earth," the puritan Henry and reformed JFB note the floating ark. Henry and JFB note that the increasing waters "bare up the ark, and it was lift up above the earth" — the rising waters lifted the ark and floated it above the earth; the ark, which had stood on the ground, was borne up by the flood; the very waters of judgment, which destroyed the world, lifted and preserved the ark and those within; the ark floated safely on the deluge.

On the lifting of the ark, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the preservation: the ark, borne up by the waters, preserved Noah and his household above the flood; the same flood that drowned the world carried the ark and the righteous safely; God's preservation of his own amid the judgment; the ark, lifted above the earth, was the refuge that rode out the deluge. The verse displays the rising flood lifting up the ark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rising — "the flood was forty days upon the earth; and the waters increased," the waters rising during the forty days (vv.11-12, Gill, Henry); "bare up the ark, and it was lift up above the earth," the rising waters floating the ark above the earth (Henry, JFB); the ark, borne up by the flood, preserving the righteous above the judgment (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the floating ark. The verse marks the flood: forty days. The verse displays the waters: increasing. The verse displays the ark: borne up. The verse displays the preservation: above the flood. The rising displays the waters lifting the ark above the earth. The verse displays the flood: rising. The verse displays the ark: lifted. The verse displays the refuge: floating. The rising flood lifting up the ark displays the preservation of the righteous amid the judgment: the flood increased and bore up the ark above the earth — the same waters that drowned the world lifting and preserving the ark and those within, the refuge riding safely on the deluge. The verse displays the floating ark, on the prevailing waters (v.18).
18:
"And the waters prevailed, and were increased greatly upon the earth; and the ark went upon the face of the waters." The very brief verse notes the waters prevailing and the ark riding upon them. The grammatical voices note the prevailing flood. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the waters prevailed, and were increased greatly upon the earth" — the waters grew mighty and increased greatly, overwhelming the earth; the flood prevailed, rising higher and spreading wider; the deluge mounted to a great height, covering the land; the irresistible increase of the waters.

On "the waters prevailed... greatly," the witnesses note the overwhelming flood. Gill notes that the waters "prevailed" — became mighty and dominant, overwhelming all; the flood was now in full force, covering the earth; the waters' increasing prevalence marks the progress of the destruction.

On "the ark went upon the face of the waters," the grammatical-historical and reformed note the floating ark: "the ark went (floated) upon the face of the waters" — the ark rode safely upon the surface of the prevailing flood; borne up by the very waters that destroyed the world, the ark floated above them; Noah and his household, in the ark, were carried safely on the face of the deluge; the refuge of God's preservation rode upon the waters of judgment. The verse displays the waters prevailing and the ark riding upon them.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the prevailing — "the waters prevailed, and were increased greatly upon the earth," the flood growing mighty and overwhelming the earth (Keil, Gill); the waters' increasing prevalence, the destruction progressing (Gill); "the ark went upon the face of the waters," the ark floating safely on the deluge, the refuge preserved (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prevailing flood. The verse marks the waters: prevailing. The verse displays the increase: greatly. The verse displays the ark: floating. The verse displays the preservation: upon the waters. The flood displays the waters overwhelming the earth while the ark rides safe. The verse displays the waters: mighty. The verse displays the ark: riding. The verse displays the refuge: borne up. The waters prevailing and the ark riding upon them display the progress of the flood and the preservation of the righteous: the waters prevailed and increased greatly, and the ark floated upon the face of the waters — the deluge overwhelming the earth while the ark, borne up by the waters of judgment, rode safely above them. The verse displays the prevailing flood, covering the high hills (v.19).
19:
"And the waters prevailed exceedingly upon the earth; and all the high hills, that were under the whole heaven, were covered." The witnesses note the waters covering all the high hills — the crux of the flood's extent. On "the waters prevailed exceedingly upon the earth," the grammatical voices note the mighty flood. Gill notes that "the waters prevailed exceedingly upon the earth" — the waters grew exceedingly mighty, rising to a great height; the flood reached its full force, covering the whole face of the earth; the waters overwhelmed all.

On "all the high hills, that were under the whole heaven, were covered," interpreters divide over the flood's extent. The reformed Gill and the traditional reading hold the flood was universal/global: "all the high hills under the whole heaven" were covered — the language teaches that the whole earth, even the highest mountains, was submerged; the flood covered the entire globe; the universal extent of the deluge, as the universal corruption (6:12) called for a universal judgment; the wesleyan-arminian Clarke defends the universality of the deluge against objectors. A second reading (the local-flood view) takes the flood as covering the known world or the region inhabited by man — the "high hills under the whole heaven" being the mountains of the region (or the whole visible horizon); on this view the flood was vast but not strictly global; Ellicott notes considerations bearing on the height (the fifteen cubits, v.20, perhaps above the mountains of the region).

On "under the whole heaven," the grammatical-historical note the comprehensiveness: "under the whole heaven" — the phrase, taken by the traditional view as marking the universal extent (the whole earth under the whole sky), is read by the local view as the whole region under the visible heaven; the universality of the language is the chief support of the global reading. The verse displays the waters covering all the high hills.

The witnesses agree the substance: the waters prevailed exceedingly and covered all the high hills under the whole heaven (Gill, Clarke, Ellicott). The extent bears a crux: a universal/global flood, covering the whole earth and the highest mountains (the traditional reading — Gill, Clarke) vs a local/regional flood, covering the known world or the region inhabited by man (the local-flood view). The verse displays the flood-extent crux. The verse marks the waters: prevailing exceedingly. The verse displays the debate: universal or local. The verse displays the hills: covered. The verse displays the heaven: the whole. The flood displays the waters covering the high hills, whether globally or regionally. The verse displays the waters: mighty. The verse displays the hills: submerged. The verse displays the extent: debated. The waters covering all the high hills display the great crux of the flood's extent: the waters prevailed exceedingly and covered all the high hills under the whole heaven — read by the traditional view as a universal, global flood covering the whole earth (Gill, Clarke), and by the local-flood view as a vast regional flood over the known world. The verse displays the covered hills, fifteen cubits deep (v.20).
20:
"Fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail; and the mountains were covered." The witnesses note the waters prevailing fifteen cubits above the mountains. On "Fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail," the grammatical voices note the depth. Clarke and Ellicott note that "fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail" — the waters rose fifteen cubits (about twenty-two feet) above the highest point; the depth was sufficient to cover the mountains; fifteen cubits was enough to float the ark over the highest peaks and to drown all that remained; the measure of the flood's prevailing height.

On "fifteen cubits upward," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the sufficiency. Clarke notes that fifteen cubits of water "were enough to drown" all the creatures; the depth above the mountains ensured the total destruction; the waters rose high enough that nothing could survive on the highest ground; the ark (thirty cubits high, drawing about fifteen) floated clear above the mountains.

On "the mountains were covered," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and witnesses note the covered heights: "the mountains were covered" — the waters covered the mountains (whether all the mountains of the globe, on the universal view, or the mountains of the region, on the local view, v.19); the fifteen cubits above the highest point ensured the covering; the flood prevailed over the highest land; the total submersion of the earth (or region) marks the completeness of the judgment. The verse displays the waters prevailing fifteen cubits above the mountains.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the depth — "Fifteen cubits upward did the waters prevail," the waters rising fifteen cubits (about twenty-two feet) above the highest point (Clarke, Ellicott); fifteen cubits enough to drown all and float the ark clear (Clarke); "the mountains were covered," the waters covering the mountains, the total submersion (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covered mountains. The verse marks the depth: fifteen cubits. The verse displays the prevailing: upward. The verse displays the mountains: covered. The verse displays the destruction: total. The flood displays the waters covering the mountains to a sufficient depth. The verse displays the cubits: fifteen. The verse displays the mountains: submerged. The verse displays the judgment: complete. The waters prevailing fifteen cubits above the mountains display the depth and completeness of the flood: fifteen cubits upward the waters prevailed, and the mountains were covered — the depth sufficient to drown all and float the ark clear above the highest land, the total submersion marking the completeness of the judgment. The verse displays the covered mountains, in which all flesh died (vv.21-22).
21:
"And all flesh died that moved upon the earth, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of beast, and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, and every man:" The witnesses note the death of all flesh in the flood. On "all flesh died that moved upon the earth," the grammatical voices note the universal death. Gill and Henry note that "all flesh died that moved upon the earth, both of fowl, and of cattle, and of beast, and of every creeping thing... and every man" — all living creatures on the earth (save those in the ark) died in the flood; the comprehensive death of fowls, cattle, beasts, creeping things, and men; the universal destruction announced (6:7, 17) is accomplished; all flesh perished.

On "both of fowl, and of cattle, and of beast, and of every creeping thing," the witnesses note the creatures. Gill notes that all the classes of air-breathing land creatures perished — birds, cattle, wild beasts, and creeping things; the whole animal creation outside the ark was swept away; the creatures, made for man, shared in his judgment.

On "and every man," the puritan Henry and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the death of mankind: "and every man" — all mankind (save the eight in the ark) perished in the flood; the corrupt race, given over to wickedness and violence (6:5, 11), was destroyed; the universal judgment fell on all men; the whole world of the ungodly perished, as Peter notes (2 Peter 2:5, "spared not the old world... bringing in the flood upon the world of the ungodly"); the wages of sin paid to the corrupt race. The verse displays the death of all flesh in the flood.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the death — "all flesh died that moved upon the earth... and every man," the universal death of all creatures and men outside the ark (6:7, 17, Gill, Henry); "both of fowl, and of cattle, and of beast, and of every creeping thing," all the classes of creatures perishing (Gill); "and every man," all mankind destroyed, the world of the ungodly (2 Peter 2:5, Henry, Wesley). The verse displays the universal death. The verse marks the flesh: all died. The verse displays the creatures: fowl, cattle, beast, creeping. The verse displays the men: every man. The verse displays the judgment: universal. The death displays all flesh outside the ark perishing in the flood. The verse displays the flesh: dead. The verse displays the creatures: destroyed. The verse displays the men: perished. The death of all flesh in the flood displays the accomplishment of the judgment: all flesh died — fowl, cattle, beast, creeping thing, and every man — the universal destruction of all that moved on the earth, save those in the ark, the corrupt world of the ungodly swept away. The verse displays the dead world, all the breathing perishing (v.22).
22:
"All in whose nostrils was the breath of life, of all that was in the dry land, died." The very brief verse notes the death of all that breathed on the dry land. The grammatical voices note the universal death. Gill and Clarke note that "All in whose nostrils was the breath of life, of all that was in the dry land, died" — every creature that breathed (had the breath of life in its nostrils), of all that lived on the dry land, died in the flood; the comprehensive death of all air-breathing creatures outside the ark; the breath of life, given by God (2:7), was taken from all the living of the land.

On "all in whose nostrils was the breath of life," the witnesses note the breathing creatures. Gill notes that "all in whose nostrils was the breath of life" — the air-breathing creatures (men and land animals); the qualification marks those that perished — the breathing creatures of the dry land; (the fish and sea-creatures, breathing in the water, are not included in the destruction).

On "of all that was in the dry land, died," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the land creatures: "of all that was in the dry land, died" — all the land-dwelling, air-breathing creatures perished; the flood destroyed the inhabitants of the dry land; only those in the ark survived; the total death of the breathing creatures of the land marks the completeness of the judgment. The verse displays the death of all that breathed on the dry land.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the death — "All in whose nostrils was the breath of life, of all that was in the dry land, died," every breathing creature of the dry land perishing (Gill, Clarke); "all in whose nostrils was the breath of life," the air-breathing creatures, men and land animals (Gill); "of all that was in the dry land, died," the total death of the land creatures (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dead land creatures. The verse marks the breath: of life. The verse displays the nostrils: of the dying. The verse displays the dry land: its creatures. The verse displays the death: total. The death displays all the breathing land creatures perishing. The verse displays the breath: taken. The verse displays the land: emptied. The verse displays the judgment: complete. The death of all that breathed on the dry land displays the completeness of the flood's destruction: all in whose nostrils was the breath of life, of all on the dry land, died — every air-breathing creature of the land perishing, the breath of life taken from all save those in the ark. The verse displays the dead land, every living substance destroyed (v.23).
23:
"And every living substance was destroyed which was upon the face of the ground, both man, and cattle, and the creeping things, and the fowl of the heaven; and they were destroyed from the earth: and Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark." The witnesses note the destruction of all living things and Noah alone remaining. On "every living substance was destroyed which was upon the face of the ground," the grammatical voices note the total destruction. Ellicott and Gill note that "every living substance was destroyed which was upon the face of the ground, both man, and cattle, and the creeping things, and the fowl of the heaven" — every living thing on the face of the ground was blotted out; the comprehensive destruction of man, cattle, creeping things, and birds; the whole order of land life (save the ark) was swept away; the universal judgment completed.

On "they were destroyed from the earth," the witnesses note the blotting out. Gill and JFB note that "they were destroyed (blotted out) from the earth" — the living creatures were removed from the earth; the destruction was total and final; the corrupt world of the living was blotted out, as God had purposed (6:7, "I will destroy... from the face of the earth").

On "Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark," the reformed JFB and grammatical-historical note the remnant: "Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark" — only Noah and those with him in the ark (the eight souls and the animals) survived; the sole remnant preserved from the universal destruction; "Noah only" marks the singular preservation of the righteous; the ark, with its few, was the sole surviving life on the earth; from this remnant the world would be renewed; the saved remnant amid the universal judgment (2 Peter 2:5). The verse displays the destruction of all living things and Noah alone remaining.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the destruction — "every living substance was destroyed... both man, and cattle, and the creeping things, and the fowl," the total destruction of all land life (Ellicott, Gill); "they were destroyed from the earth," the blotting out of the living, as God purposed (6:7, Gill, JFB); "Noah only remained alive, and they that were with him in the ark," the sole remnant preserved, the saved few (2 Peter 2:5, JFB, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the surviving remnant. The verse marks the destruction: every living substance. The verse displays the blotting: from the earth. The verse displays the remnant: Noah only. The verse displays the ark: the survivors. The destruction displays all life blotted out save Noah and the ark. The verse displays the living: destroyed. The verse displays the Noah: remaining. The verse displays the ark: surviving. The destruction of all living things and Noah alone remaining display the completed judgment and the preserved remnant: every living substance was destroyed from the earth, and Noah only remained alive, with those in the ark — the universal destruction of all land life, and the sole surviving remnant in the ark, from whom the world would be renewed. The verse displays the surviving Noah, the waters prevailing 150 days (v.24).
24:
"And the waters prevailed upon the earth an hundred and fifty days." The very brief verse notes the waters prevailing a hundred and fifty days. The grammatical voices note the duration of the flood. Gill and Ellicott note that "the waters prevailed upon the earth an hundred and fifty days" — the waters prevailed (remained at their height, or continued mighty) upon the earth for a hundred and fifty days; the flood, after the forty days' rain, continued to prevail for the full period; the long duration of the flood, the waters covering the earth for five months.

On "an hundred and fifty days," the witnesses note the period. Gill and Clarke note that the hundred and fifty days (including the forty days of rain, or reckoned from the beginning) mark the period during which the waters prevailed at their full height before they began to abate (8:3); the long months during which the earth lay under the flood; the duration marks the thoroughness of the judgment and the trial of Noah's faith and patience in the ark.

On the prevailing waters, the reformed and grammatical-historical note the preservation: through the hundred and fifty days, Noah and his household, and the creatures, were preserved in the ark, borne up on the prevailing waters; God's care sustained the remnant through the long flood; the waters prevailed, but the ark and its few survived; the flood ran its appointed course before God remembered Noah and made the waters to abate (8:1). The verse displays the waters prevailing a hundred and fifty days.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the duration — "the waters prevailed upon the earth an hundred and fifty days," the flood prevailing for a hundred and fifty days (Gill, Ellicott); the long period of the waters at their height before abating (8:3, Gill, Clarke); through the period the ark and its few preserved (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prevailing flood. The verse marks the waters: prevailing. The verse displays the duration: 150 days. The verse displays the earth: covered. The verse displays the preservation: of the remnant. The duration displays the flood prevailing for five months over the earth. The verse displays the waters: at their height. The verse displays the days: 150. The verse displays the ark: preserved. The waters prevailing a hundred and fifty days display the long duration of the flood: the waters prevailed upon the earth a hundred and fifty days — the deluge continuing at its height for five months, the earth lying under the waters, while the ark and its few were preserved, before God remembered Noah and the waters abated. The verse closes the account of the rising flood, before the waters' decrease (ch.8).
